rows.title: IMY Sets 2026 Supervisory Priorities: AI in Public Sector, Children's Data and Law Enforcement Tools
rows.summary:
The Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) has published its supervisory and guidance priorities for 2026, identifying AI use in the public sector, data protection for children and young people, and law enforcement tools as the three focus areas under the GDPR.

On 23 February 2026, IMY announced that these priority areas were selected following the authority's assessment that they present elevated privacy risks or that increased knowledge is needed to improve compliance. No financial penalties are associated with this priority-setting announcement.

IMY finds that the public sector's growing adoption of AI warrants particular attention because citizens engaging with public services frequently have no alternative provider to turn to, making it harder to avoid data processing by AI systems. Where those systems handle sensitive personal data, IMY finds that heightened demands apply with respect to privacy protection (Art. 9 GDPR). During 2026, IMY states it will follow up on how public bodies use AI and will provide support to help them deploy the technology in a manner consistent with data protection obligations.

On children and young people, IMY notes that minors are frequent users of digital services, including the internet, social media and apps, often without a full understanding of the associated risks. IMY further notes that children's personal data merits specific protection under the GDPR (Recital 38 GDPR). The authority states it will work to raise awareness among young people themselves and among those who process data on their behalf, including guardians and schools.

Regarding law enforcement, IMY finds that certain tools used by law enforcement agencies, including secret coercive measures and the collection of biometric data, involve restrictions on individuals' right to privacy. IMY states that its 2026 supervision and guidance activity will examine these tools from a data protection perspective. Director General Eric Leijonram is quoted as stating that IMY prioritises areas where the authority's efforts can make the most difference, reflecting its assessment that risks are greater in some areas than others.

rows.title: IMY Fines Sportadmin SEK 6 Million for Inadequate Security Following Data Breach Affecting 2.1 Million Individuals
rows.summary:
Sweden's Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten (IMY) has imposed an administrative fine of SEK 6 million (approximately EUR 530,000) on sports administration platform Sportadmin following a cyber attack that exposed personal data relating to more than 2.1 million individuals, finding a violation of Article 32 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

On 28 January 2026, the Swedish Authority for Privacy Protection (IMY) published its decision against Sportadmin i Skandinavien AB, imposing a fine of SEK 6 million (approximately EUR 530,000) after a supervisory review initiated in response to a cyber attack that occurred in January 2025. IMY finds that the attacker gained access to data concerning more than 2.1 million individuals and subsequently published that data on the Darknet.

IMY finds that the compromised data primarily concerned children and young people and included names, contact details, personal identity numbers, and information about the sport and sports club each individual was associated with. IMY's decision notes that the leaked data also included sensitive health data and, to some extent, data relating to persons with protected identity, meaning individuals whose personal data is held as confidential. IMY's review identified both technical and organisational deficiencies at Sportadmin. IMY finds that, for a period prior to the attack, Sportadmin was aware of certain weaknesses in its systems and of areas carrying elevated risks of attack, and is deemed not to have done enough to address them.

IMY further finds that Sportadmin lacked the routines required to detect deficiencies in existing security measures and did not operate a system capable of detecting intrusions and attempted intrusions in real time (Art. 32 GDPR). IMY determines that, had such measures been in place, Sportadmin would have been better positioned to prevent the incident or limit the resulting damage. IMY concludes that Sportadmin's failure to maintain a level of security appropriate to the personal data it processed constitutes a violation of Article 32 GDPR, providing the basis for the SEK 6 million administrative fine.
